sentences.text: Buna.
Indeed.
Of the camp.
Of a number of barracks, a portion of the camp.
Yeah.
That's right.
They were brought there for a limited period of time.
Yeah.
Yeah, escaped.
I took it over.
Yeah, the Gestapo.
Because SS men had nothing to do with my camp.
Only the-- in spite, it was the whole camp was SS.
But the top, I mean, the people responsible for this camp was the Gestapo.
And I had about approximately 450 to 600 people coming and going.
After months they were released.
Maybe the other one had six weeks, or eight weeks, you know.
It went on and off.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
For instance, when I released them, and after a week again they show up.
So tell me, what are you doing here?
You just left.
Why are you coming here?
They told me, Doctor, we have it with you better here than outside.
I treated them well.
I went to the kitchen.
I told the bosses in the kitchen, listen, I have young people.
They're all Poles, and Czechs, and all those.
They are hungry.
Give me more.
So instead say six kettles of soup, I got eight.
So I could give some more.
Instead a certain amount of breads, they gave me more breads, see?
But what I did mostly in the evening, when people came out from work back to the camp-- well, not only from my camp, but from the whole, big camp-- my friends came.
And I gave them food.
I gave them clothes.
I helped them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I was only interested in make it easier life for everyone, see?
I was safe.
Safe?
They could call me out every second and kill me, you know.
It came to December, '44.
We knew already the war is over because one of the guards who at night guards around my camp, we got to talk to.
A German.
And he said, you know, you seem to be a very nice man.
I know you're Jewish, but what's the different?
You suffer.
And you know what?
The war is almost over.
Maybe you run way, you want to run away to escape.
I'll give you a gun.
SS man.
I said, no.
I don't need a gun, because I don't intend to run away.
Where shall I go?
It's all occupied by you, by the Germans.
I don't need the gun.
And we were talking, talking.
And it was almost the end.
I'm called to the SS, to the Gestapo again.
And he was a very nice man.
He was a sergeant, but more intelligent than the other guards.
He was in charge of the Gestapo.
And he--
His name was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I might tell you later on.
I forgot.
And he was the only one I ever came together with Germans who never called me by my name only.
But he called me Herr, Sir.
And I don't recall, I think in the whole, during the war, there was not one Jew who was called in the concentration camps, Herr.
He respected me.
And that day in December he calls me.
He said, you know, Mr.
Halbreich, I have to release you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why?
I get questionnaires from Berlin, from the headquarter.
What's going on in the relocation camp?
People are coming and going so often.
What's the matter?
I said, you know, Mr.-- you know better than.
I do my job.
Do you have complaints about me?
He said, no.
I have no complaints about you, but they ask me to replace you.
Where do you want to go?
Normally, he could send me to a coal mine, to Auschwitz-Birkenau, where they would finish me up, you know?
And he ask me, where do you want to go?
I said naturally I want to go to the hospital barrack.
I went back to the hospital.
And then my replacement was the doctor who was my assistant.
He was the one.
So we worked all together all the time, you know.
And I worked in the hospital the last two months.
I was opening-- installing a new post-surgery department, a new barrack.
And then another barrack for reconvalescence.
See, this never, in no camp was possible.
But the IG Farben industry, the Buna-- this is a factory, who you know it now, IG Farben Industry-- this is the largest chemical plant in the world, up today.
And they were interested in people.
They needed labor.
So they started hospital, because three years in Sachsenhausen and Gross-Rosen there were hospitals, but not for Jews.
Never could the Jew go to hospital.
Our people died in the factory, on the job, in the barracks.
And then we carried some out to the crematoriums.
This was it.
Very important thing is-- this I have to mention.
In 1941, in June, when the Russians entered-- I mean, no, when the Germans were attacking the Russians-- they broke the agreement, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the non-war agreements.
They had peace, a contract, an agreement with the Germans not to attack each other, because they divided Poland.
Half they took, half they took, you see?
And they just disregarded it, attacked the Russians.
And they brought Russian prisoner of wars to the camps every day, by the thousands, young, Russian soldiers.
And at night there was a curfew.
We couldn't get out of the barracks.
And we heard only shooting all night.
They killed them all.
In the morning we Jewish prisoners, we had to form groups.
And we had to carry the Russian bodies from the place where they were killed to the crematoriums.
And this went on for weeks and weeks.
Thousands and 10,000s of Russians.
After the war I find out this does not happen just in Sachsenhausen, but in all other German camps it's the same thing.
They never arrested them or put them in prisoner of war camps.
Never.
The Russians, they were killed, all.
This was Auschwitz.
The last two months in Auschwitz I worked in the hospital.
And about January 17th the Russians came closer.
We heard already the cannons, the shooting.
And normally they would destroy the camps, kill all the people, but they didn't have time.
The Russians were so close.
So they decided to transport us.
And they assembled 65,000 men and women from all 39 camps in Silesia and they put us on a death march.
We marched first two days.
And then in Gleiwitz-- this is German city in Silesia-- they supplied us with trains, but not, naturally, for all.
And the other ones had to all march.
And I got on a train.
But during the march already, they killed from 65,000, 25,000 people in ten days.
Killing by marching, freezing to death, starving to death, just giving up.
They couldn't make it anymore, sick people.
And they were just-- with the guns they killed them.
Some tried to escape.
Naturally, they caught them.
They killed them.
And 45,000 remained.
And we were distributed.
We marched day and night for weeks.
In Germany they came to one camp.
Was overfilled.
They didn't accept them.
They had to go farther.
It was just horrible.
I was 10 days on the train.
When I got on the train I made a big sign that I put a red cross on.
And I occupied the first car.
And they put about 15 to 18 people on the car.
They were not sick.
I had blankets and they were laying.
It was an open car, in the winter snowing, you know, freezing.
All other cars were occupied with hundreds of people.
They had no place to sit down.
They were standing.
And naturally, a lot of people were dying.
And the people were throwing overboard in order to have more room.
Only once during the ten days we stopped, and they gave us some food, a little piece of sausage with a piece of bread.
And I was the only one on the train who could go down from the train, because I had my arm band saying I'm the camp oldest of the relocation camp.
I didn't take it away.
I kept it.
And I could go down, and I helped people, giving water.
First of all, they have snow.
So they were not thirsty, but giving water.
The population of Czechoslovakia, they tried to come close to the train and giving us food.
They chased them away, couldn't.
And one time only they distributed food.
They asked every car, how many in the car?
They asked me how many.
I said I'm 35.
I got double, you know?
And this way I went from train to train.
I saw a friend, I give the friends the food, you know?
And this is all in my book, testifying in my appendix, by the people writing about it, how I helped, how I handled the time, you know?
And we came to-- after about ten days going by train we arrived in Nordhausen.
In Nordhausen, again, out of the train, marching up into the mountains.
And there was Dora.
Dora camp was situated on the mountains, a huge camp.
There I remained, I would say, maybe five days.
Again, undressing, taking showers, getting new clothes.
You become a new prisoner, you know.
Start from the beginning.
Torn shoes, a pair of pants, too big.
I had to hold my pants, I shouldn't drop my pants.
I couldn't get into the jacket.
Was too narrow, too small.
And then we had to exchange one with another one, you know?
But it was terrible.
We didn't do nothing.
Again we formed a transport, about, I would say, 400 people, mostly of our camp from Auschwitz.
Even our camp oldest, from the whole camp, was with us too.
Was a German criminal, a German, but he was a very, very nice man.
We could deal with him.
Especially-- in Buna we were 90% Jews and we took over the camp.
There were-- all administration of the camp, they were all Jews.
In the hospital, all Jews.
All the block elders, they were mostly all Jews.
So we consider now, after the war, that this was the best-run camp of all camps in occupied Europe, because we were running it, you see?
And then we had, naturally, an underground resistance group.
This was in Nordhausen?
No.
It's in Auschwitz already.
In Auschwitz, yeah.
In the underground, yes.
Naturally, we couldn't take in all the people.
We selected people who were the most trustworthy, who were a long time in camp, each other knew.
We were maybe six, seven.
The main secretary of the main camp was a Jew from Austria, from Vienna.
In the hospital was a Polish doctor who was in charge of the hospital.
There was my friend, who is still alive in Warsaw, who was a communist, a Jew, who was in the hospital.
And he was the man in charge of the whole underground.
A very smart man.
At that time his name was Eliezar Silman.
I gave it to you yesterday.
He changed after the war his name to Leo Staszak.
He's a Pole.
I will tell you later on why.
And he was the main man.
When we had any questions-- so to perform a job, a secret job, we went always with him for advice.
And he was leading the group of communists, the Jewish group of communists.
Young people, old people, doesn't matter.
And I was leading the Zionists.
We arranged escapes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were hiding people when they were sick.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We committed sabotage in the factory.
In the Buna factory.
For instance, first of all, we did the ground work.
Then they had to build the different plants, the factories.
The machinery came in.
And one day, it was in October, '44, the factory was ready.
So the Germans invited the big shots from Berlin to come for the official opening.
And they arrived.
And here the engineer, the main engineer, goes to the electric switch and want it to start, and show them how it works.
It didn't start.
Why?
Because our people working in the factory, they put sand in the oil pipes.
It didn't work.
They were, naturally, very disgusted, you know?
They couldn't find a reason.
They found it.
It took another few weeks, another few months.
And this, to my knowledge, twice happened.
The factory never worked, never started to work.
This was our work, in combination with the Polish secret underground, with the English prisoners of wars.
With the English prisoner of wars we exchanged.
They were short on blankets, on sheets.
So we supplied to them.
How?
When the kettles were filled with soup in the kitchen, and brought to the factory at lunchtime, we filled up two, three, the kettles with blankets, with sheets, and it was carried out.
And vice versa.
When they emptied it, they put in for us, for instance, fruit, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sausages, sardines, chocolate.
They got it from the Red Cross, the English prisoner of wars, you see?
So we exchanged it.
One day, we took an English officer.
He changed the uniform, our uniform.
He put on our uniform.
And one of our prisoners put his uniform on.
He went with them to their camp.
And he came for two days to our camp, just to find out what's going on in the camp.
Because those English prisoner of wars, they had radios, naturally hidden.
And they were in touch with England.
And they told them everything what was going on.
And from them we got the information.
But not all prisoners knew about it.
Only the group, the secrets of the underground, the secret service, the resistance.
So there was always a fight.
We didn't give up.
It is not true that the Jews went to death like, how you call it?
They call it like-- like sheeps.
There was resistance.

Even the resistance I mentioned to you-- the people who were designated to go to the selections, to the gas chambers-- they had the strength yet to take off their clothes and leave it for other ones, just to help other ones, you see?
This is all resistance, because physically we couldn't resist.
We had no arms.
We couldn't fight in our camps.
Naturally, you know about Sobibor, the resistance.
Or in Auschwitz.
Later on in Auschwitz too, in Birkenau, where people who were serving in the gas chambers revolted.
Or the mines.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mentioning about the mining.
My sister was working in the factory, in the ammunition factory.
And from there they smuggled out in their handkerchiefs powder and gave it to the people working in the crematoriums and the gas chambers.
They made the bombs out of it.
Four girls were caught and were immediately hung.
We were already on the transport going out of Auschwitz when they caught the four girls.
They killed them.
My sister, they didn't catch.
And other ones, not.
My sister went on the transport like we, and was put in a car, in a train.
And while the train was still standing, she noticed the other train on the other [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] railroad track, there were friends.
And they waved her over.
And she jumped over to the other train.
And the Germans were looking all over.
Already by fleeing, by going, by retreating, finding the other girls, they couldn't find her, because my sister was jumping from one train to another.
And she survived.
And fine, this I see, maybe Auschwitz, there are many things more.
But what else can I tell you about Auschwitz?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You have to know when a prisoner is being promoted, say he became a block elders, or a kapo, or a-- camp oldest was very seldom.
I remember I was the only one, the only Jew.
So they respect authority, the Germans.
It's a military nation.
And when see prisoner has an arm band, he is something special.
So they treat you different too.
They don't beat you.
And they let you go out from one camp to another one, with a guard, naturally.

But I was able-- myself and the doctor, the main doctor from the hospital-- we were able to go out of the camp, across the highway to the factory, because this was all guarded, a big chain of SS men were guarding it.
But they let us out from the camp.
Only we both would go, nobody else.
And we took-- naturally, we took advantage of it.
One day the gardener of the main camp, a Jew, comes to my camp and says, Sig, you have regards from your cousin.
Who is my cousin?
Genya Halbreich.
I said, you know, I don't know a Genya Halbreich.
This is not my cousin.
I don't know her.
But you know what, I will try next time-- when you go to this camp where the women are-- I will go with you.
How will you do it?
I will work out something.
I see the man who was in charge, the SS man, you know, who was friendly with me.
I said, Neubert, come out, I want to show you something.
We're coming out, and I said, you see, this is our hospital.
We have five, six, seven, barracks.
But you cannot-- there's no difference between the main camp and the hospital.
We should little clean it up and do something.
What do you have in mind?
I said why don't we take some bushes or plant or grass and make it nicer so it will look like a hospital.
He said, how can we do it?
I said, you see, [?
Jup, ?] the gardener goes every week to the camp, to Raisko.
This was a woman camp.
They planted there.
They had vegetables and fruit for the SS men, for the SS.
I said, he goes every week there, and brings for the SS men things.
I could go with him.
Well, you can go.
Fine, I'll let you go.
I told him, listen, I can go with you.
Let me know when.
The following week he comes back.
I can go.
So I went up on truck with SS men, naturally, the guard, always him.
And we came to this camp.
And we come in.
And all of a sudden, first time in all four years, I see girls, women, you know?
They come running to me.
And I said, I want to talk to Genya Halbreich.
And here comes a girl, a beauty.
Tall, black hair.
That time, the women already grow short hair.
And she comes.
And I said I'm Sig Halbreich.
I'm Genya Halbreich.
And she looks at me, and she said, come with me.
And she takes me to the office.
There is an SS man, the man in charge, sitting there.
And she said Mr.
So and So, this is my cousin.
He looks at me, he gets up from the chair, walks out in the front room, and lets us both alone for an hour.
And I talked to her and I found out that she is married to a very distant cousin of mine.
I never knew him, even from Krakow.
And we tell each other from where we are, what we're doing, where our parents are.
So shortly, and she said from now on, we'll keep in touch.
This gardener every time brought from her fresh vegetables, fruit for me, you know, lettuce.
Between this camp and this camp you had communication with main camp, Auschwitz, you know.
From [INAUDIBLE].
I found out there are people there.
And we had communications.
And she was a middle person, because he transported and brought with the letters.
We had to be very careful.
The letters, he was hiding in the flower pot, see?
Then, in the meantime, we put grass on there.
So this was the way the resistance worked, you see?
I was the second time there.
